locked_facts: Range R = u²sin(2θ)/g — depends on sin of double the angle
Maximum range occurs at θ = 45° for a given speed on level ground
Complementary angles θ and (90°−θ) give the same range: sin(2θ) = sin(2(90°−θ))
At maximum height, vertical velocity = 0 but horizontal velocity = u·cos(θ)
truth_statements: The range is maximum at 45° because sin(2θ) is maximum (=1) when 2θ = 90°
Two complementary angles of projection give the same range but different maximum heights and times of flight
The maximum height for θ = 90°−α is always greater than for θ = α when α < 45°
Horizontal and vertical motions are independent — horizontal velocity remains u·cos(θ) throughout
Time of flight depends only on the vertical component u·sin(θ), not on horizontal velocity

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two projectiles launched simultaneously at complementary angles (e.g. 30° and 60°) — both land at exactly the same horizontal distance but follow very different paths. The 60° path is taller and slower, the 30° path is flatter and faster.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Both trajectories overlaid on the same axes with the landing points coinciding perfectly — range equality proven visually
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume a higher angle always gives a longer range. Seeing two different angles produce the same range reveals the sin(2θ) symmetry.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A projectile launcher on the ground with adjustable angle indicator. The initial velocity vector is shown decomposed into u·cos(θ) horizontal and u·sin(θ) vertical components. Formulas for R, H, T displayed on a panel.
simulation_states.emphasis: Clear decomposition of initial velocity into horizontal and vertical components

simulation_states.label: Single Launch
simulation_states.description: Projectile launched at 45° traces a parabolic path. Range R, maximum height H, and time of flight T are measured and displayed. The values match the formulas exactly.
simulation_states.emphasis: At 45°, sin(2θ) = 1, giving maximum range for this speed. Show computed vs measured values matching.

simulation_states.label: Complementary Angles
simulation_states.description: Two projectiles launched simultaneously at complementary angles (30° and 60°) with the same speed. Both trajectories drawn — they land at the same point. Height and time of flight differ but range is identical.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same range, different heights — complementary angle symmetry. sin(60°) = sin(120°) = sin(2×30°) = sin(2×60°).

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode: student adjusts launch angle and initial speed. Range, height, and time update live. A second ghost trajectory at the complementary angle is always shown for comparison. A bar chart shows R vs θ (peaks at 45°).
simulation_states.emphasis: Experiment with angles on both sides of 45° to verify complementary angle theorem. See the R vs θ curve peak at 45°.

common_misconceptions.belief: A higher angle always gives a longer range
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT shows range R = u²sin(2θ)/g. Range increases with θ only up to 45°, then decreases. At 90° range is zero (ball goes straight up and comes back).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show R vs θ graph peaking at 45° and dropping to zero at 90°

common_misconceptions.belief: 45° always gives the maximum range regardless of conditions
common_misconceptions.correction: 45° gives max range only on level ground with no air resistance. On an inclined plane or with air drag, the optimal angle differs. NCERT derivation assumes level ground.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Demonstrate 45° optimality on level ground, then note the assumption

common_misconceptions.belief: Complementary angles give the same maximum height
common_misconceptions.correction: Complementary angles give the same range but NOT the same height. H = u²sin²(θ)/(2g), so the steeper angle always reaches a greater height.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two trajectories at 30° and 60° — same range but very different peak heights
